Newborn Weight Loss Calculator — % Loss in Lbs, Oz & Grams

Newborns typically lose 5–10% of birth weight in the first 3–5 days. More than 10% signals a feeding concern that needs immediate attention. Enter your baby's birth weight and current weight to get the exact percentage — and a clear answer on whether it's within the safe range.

What Is Physiological Weight Loss in Newborns?

Newborn weight loss in the first days of life is a normal biological process. Babies shed excess fluid accumulated in the womb, pass meconium (first stool), and adjust to feeding outside the uterus. This is called physiological weight loss, and it is expected in all healthy newborns regardless of feeding method.

According to the American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) 2024 guidelines, a weight loss of up to 10% of birth weight is within the range that can be monitored at home with close follow-up. More than 10% triggers clinical evaluation to rule out inadequate milk transfer, dehydration, or latching problems.

Normal Weight Loss by Feeding Method

Feeding Method Normal Loss Range Concern Threshold Regain by Day
Breastfed 5–7% >10% 10–14 days
Formula-fed 3–5% >7% 7–10 days
Mixed feeding 4–6% >8% 8–12 days

Source: American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) Newborn Nutrition Guidelines, 2024.

When to Call Your Pediatrician

Contact your baby's doctor immediately if:

  • Weight loss exceeds 10% of birth weight at any point
  • Your baby has not regained birth weight by 14 days of age
  • Your baby shows signs of dehydration (fewer than 6 wet diapers per day by day 4)
  • Your baby appears lethargic, jaundiced, or is difficult to wake for feedings
  • Breastfeeding is painful or you are unsure the baby is latching correctly

For more context, see our newborn weight loss guide and the baby weight loss calculator for tracking progress beyond the newborn period.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: What is a normal weight loss percentage for a newborn?

A loss of 5–7% is normal for breastfed newborns. Up to 10% is the clinical monitoring threshold. Formula-fed babies typically lose 3–5%. Any loss above 10% requires evaluation by a pediatrician.

Q: When should a baby regain their birth weight?

Most healthy newborns regain their birth weight by 10–14 days of age. Premature infants may take slightly longer. If your baby has not returned to birth weight by 2 weeks, contact your pediatrician promptly.

Q: Is 10% weight loss dangerous for a newborn?

A 10% loss is a clinical alert — not automatically an emergency, but it requires immediate assessment of feeding adequacy, latch quality, and hydration status. Over 10% is the threshold that triggers supplemental feeding recommendations from the AAP.
